Hey guys, welcome back to this lesson. This is where we are really going to go deeper into what exactly that transformation is between point A and point B. I think any journey or transformation sounds kind of vague until you qualify it until you actually really outline that map, outline the steps someone needs to take. So this is where we're really actually going to get a little more granular and talk about what those key milestones are along that buyer's journey and how to start using our messaging to generate momentum, build that momentum and move someone, move a prospect through that path between point A and point buyer. So here is that path once again. And if you look at the bottom of our map here, you will see that point A, the first milestone is really validated. That's the entry ticket to getting a prospect on this journey with us is to make them feel validated in their current experience. Get them to a place where they feel seen, heard and understood. It's because you've confirmed their current reality to them, including the things they're already trying to do to achieve that outcome or overcome that pain. You know, reflecting that, validating that, recognizing that is what makes them trust you enough to embark on that journey. Trust you enough that you've taken all of them into consideration and still can promise this new favorable outcome to them. So that's what we're doing here and built into this initial phase of validating, of achieving that first milestone. It's impossible to not have a conversation about your prospect's level of awareness. And these are the classic five levels of awareness by Gene Schwartz. No relation, not that I know of anyway. But essentially, in the coaching online education space that a lot of you are operating under, it's my experience that most of your prospects are going to fall under the problem aware level. However, there is a slight caveat that really fuels my initial research, my initial deep dive into an audience. And it's leaning in with the question, is anybody ever really problem aware? You know, I never take for granted that my prospect is even problem aware. In fact, even if they are classically considered to be problem aware, I like to approach my campaigns with this, you know, with this beginner's mind and tap into what I consider to be a hidden sixth stage of awareness or a sixth level. And that is symptom aware. You know, no one really has a true grasp on the source of their problem. You know, they have an idea, they may have kind of a precognition of it, a conception, but especially in the online space, in the online education space coaching, consulting, the root problem is your job as the expert to elucidate, to educate. And that's a very important gift you can give them, especially at the beginning of a funnel, where you're validating their experience and giving them that gift of diagnosis. You know, it's kind of like when you go to a doctor and saying, you know, this hurts and that hurts, you know, my stomach has been hurting me for weeks, that's a symptom that that's not a problem. You know, an inexperienced doctor or a friend might consider your stomach hurting to be the problem, but that's actually symptomatic of something much deeper. And the truth is ultimately, every pain point is both a problem and a symptom. And if you really want to make your product, your course, your program, the only logical choice, it all starts with diagnosing the real problem. So operate from a place that your prospect is symptomatically aware, not problem aware, and then give them that gift of diagnosis. You know, that's your job as your educator, your job is to give that gift of perfect diagnosis and we'll show you how to do that in your messaging. Once we get into the prelaunch sequence. But for now, I just want you to consider this secret sixth stage, because that is where I've operated in every launch I've done for clients is really tapping into how can I turn what my prospect considers to be a problem and actually make it symptomatic of a more root problem that we're uniquely capable of solving, because that's a very powerful relationship you can create with your audience. You know, that is how you build authority. It's not just about having articles up on Forbes on entrepreneur on Inc. or whatever the main publications of your industry are, you know, anybody could do that. But being able to expertly diagnose and peel back and say, no, that's actually a symptom of something deeper. That is really how you not just tell people you're an authority with some media logos, but actually show it display it without having to boast. So this is kind of how that process goes for that hidden sixth stage. And we will get into an exercise, I'll walk you through an exercise a little later on in this module on how to uncover it. But it's essentially taking what what they perceive what your audience perceives to be a problem, taking a few steps back and really saying that that's actually a symptom, which gives you the right to read diagnose the real problem. And if backed up by believable data or case studies, it actually makes you the sole authority to solve it, you know, you've created this new, this new relationship between your prospect and the problem they're feeling, and that gives you the unique right to solve it in your way. So that is one major thing that I really credit as being probably the most powerful shift I've made as a copywriter as a marketer is to just not take people's perceived problems for granted, and actually hold their hand as we dive deeper and make it a symptom instead of a problem and then unearth root out the real problem, which makes us really the only people capable of solving it. So that's how you kind of create a blue ocean around your product, you really change the problem, you redefine the problem in a way that only you could solve it. So there's one other thing I want to talk about while we're still on the topic of Uncle Gene, and that's the difference between levels of awareness and depth of awareness. And this is something that probably wasn't as irrelevant to be talking about back in the 50s, you know, or 60s or 70s or 80s or even the 90s. But right now, you know, when our attention is so scattered and pulled at by so many different areas, you know, it's not enough to really just focus on what is your prospects level of awareness, you really need to ramp up and amplify their depth of awareness, you know, just because someone is symptomatically aware or problem aware doesn't mean their awareness over it is strong or consistent enough for them to be motivated enough to move through it. So just as just as it's important to identify the level of awareness, holding awareness over it long enough to get them to act is also part of your job as a skillful marketer or a skillful conversion coach. And it's kind of like reflecting sun through a magnifying glass, you know, you really need to hold it there long enough until it catches fire until that problem really starts to burn until that symptom is really that main focus. So it's really holding that focus that depth of awareness. And to do this, I like to marry our good friend Jean Schwartz with a little guy named Freud. So definitely no relation to Freud. And we're just kind of using his name, his name to kind of break down these five points, if you will. But this is how I ramp up the, this is how I ramp up that depth of awareness, you know, and this is something that really gets dialed in over the course of your funnel. And it all starts with frequency, you know, this is why during that intensive period of a launcher, when someone enters your funnel, you want to be mailing them daily, you want them to see you at different points. This is why we do retargeting. This is why you'll send emails with great frequency, but you'll also send maybe a text as a reminder, if somebody registered for your webinar, that's why you'll also try to send retargeting ads to just stay top of mind. So frequency is really important. It's not enough to just capture attention, you have to stay in front of them long enough to hold it, or at least during that period of transformation. You know, we're not saying that you're going to be in front of them eight times a day for the next year, we're saying be in front of them as frequently as possible during that one week, two week, three week period of your launch. Because when you think about it, if you're trying to create an incredible personal transformation for yourself, and you're working with a coach, or you're working with a therapist, what do you do? You know, a lot of people go on those weekend intensives, where you're literally spending 72 straight hours with a group, you know, it has that frequency effect. If you're really trying to work on a new habit or behavior or overcoming some sort of trauma, you might have a lot of sessions in a really short span, because you're really focused on trying to create that transformation. So the other thing is repetition, you know, practically speaking, this means choosing a few powerful sales arguments and beliefs and reframes to make across your funnel, instead of throwing around more and more and more to see what sticks. You know, as we get deeper into the course, especially during the sales module and module six, you'll see, you'll see what we're actually doing there. You know, you'll see how we're really setting up those arguments in the pre-launch and reinforcing them in the sales phases instead of creating new ones. So the goal is really to repeat them, strengthen them, reinforce them and recontextualize them instead of diluting and muddying the pool with just too many ideas. The other thing is being evocative, you know, that captures attention. That means using story, using word pictures, really leveraging specificity in your copy to create this vivid image in your prospect's mind. You know, what captures our attention, what captures the depth of attention is when we actually use more of our faculties to relate with it. You know, our brains don't know the difference between an imagined experience and a real one. So if you can walk them through and really be evocative in your words, that's what gets your prospects to play full, to increase that depth of awareness, that depth of attention, and that's something that is extremely strong and something you want to capitalize on over the course of that funnel. And the other one is unexpected, you know, that's your classic pattern interrupt. And, you know, we're going to learn how to do this right off the bat early in our funnel by calling out the sacred cows. You know, that could be a little bit constrain, that gets the ears perking, you know, so it's really curiosity driven. But it's also a good idea, you know, to know what your prospect is seeing, you know, how they've been trying to solve the problem so you can effectively position against that white noise. So you can call BS on the things that they're aware of, you know, and really draw that attention. So unexpected sometimes means being challenging, you know, it's just anything that really separates and distances from the white noise of your market and gets that attention. Because once it once they just kind of hook on to the fact that you're approaching things in a different unexpected way, that's what's going to earn you the right to kind of, you know, earn the right to continue communicating with them and leveraging that attention. So that's a big one. And then finally, probably the most powerful one, especially for the coaches, the experts, the educators, the trainers listening to this, but be diagnostic, you know, and the way I like to illustrate this is think of when you as, you know, a normal human, not the entrepreneurial version of you, is the most focused and dialed in and present with what someone is saying, you know, it might not be your spouse or your partner when they're talking about their day. But if you just went through a series of medical testing for something and you had those 15 minutes, you know, sitting in front of a doctor when they come back into the room and, you know, they have those results and they're about to kind of really dive into it with you and tell you exactly what is up, you're paying attention, you know, you are, your ears are perked up and you are focusing on every word. So the more diagnostic you could be across your funnel, especially at the beginning, that is how time almost becomes suspended. That's how you become the most important person in their life in that moment. So we're talking about diagnosing everything, diagnosing previous failures, diagnosing their last action, you know, if they clicked on a link, what does that mean? If they signed up for a webinar but didn't attend, what does that mean about what's going on in their life? So definitely diagnose wherever possible. That's what creates those, oh my God, he gets me moments when you can reflect back and diagnose exactly why someone did something because they haven't actually done any of the self-awareness to know why they did it themselves. So that is extremely powerful and that really blends well with validating their initial starting point and what they're seeing, you know, that comes down to diagnosis. So that's really a loaded deep overview of that first milestone. And as we go deeper, the second one is simply excitement around the outcome, you know, most afflicted prospects will start off with a sense of disillusionment and skepticism around solving the problem or achieving the outcome, you know, but if you validated their reality and diagnosed why they haven't succeeded in the past, it opens up this small window of hope, you know, the shell cracks just wide enough to get your prospect excited about the outcome again. They may still be skeptical and that's okay, they're allowed to be skeptical, but we have their attention and we have their excitement, which is really what we're going for at that stage of the customer journey. And there are other ways to build that excitement. And we're going to talk more about them as we get into module three, but one of them is a concept I call exclusive empowerment. And this is where you give your prospect the chance to cash in on their privilege points. You know, we all have this drive to make the most out of our potential and opportunity, no one wants to leave privilege points on the table. So this is where you give them a chance to leverage it, leverage their current skills or circumstantial elements, you know, if your method or what you're going to do is only going to work for entrepreneurs who've earned over 150k in the past year, that's something they're proud of, that's something they want to leverage. So it's kind of like a velvet rope policy, but taken to a deeper, more meaningful level and we'll get more into that later. You know, in Joseph Campbell's Pure's journey, this is where we would call them to adventure. You know, there's a sense of being the chosen one and it has to be them. You know, they've been selected and that's a very powerful trigger to elicit excitement. It is exciting, leveraging opportunities that are given to you. And the other thing we're really setting up here is the sense of it's not your fault until it is. You know, this is a big theme I've used in every single launch. You know, what gets people re-engaged and excited about an outcome is the transference of blame or self-judgment that comes along with it. You know, they may have been embarrassed or ashamed that they're still spinning their wheels on a problem, but part of your job, especially in the early going, it's to show them why it wasn't their fault in a believable way that they could actually buy into, you know, and this is what takes the energy out of self-blame and allows them to channel it back into resourcefulness and excitement. And it gives you a chance, more importantly, to keep them accountable towards the end. This is why I call it the it's not your fault until it is principal. Because once they see the light, once they buy into it and commit to it, it's their responsibility to act on it. And it's your responsibility as a conversion coach to challenge them to stay consistent with that. So once we have that excitement, we want to get our prospect committed to that new outcome. And we want to get that commitment in the most friction-free part of your funnel. And that's the important note here. There's no offer yet at this stage of the buyer's journey, only the promise of an exciting outcome, only forgiveness over past failures. You know, so that creates hope that it's achievable and a commitment towards achieving it. And this is exactly what you want to dial in way before your offer comes into play. You know, you want to get that commitment to the outcome before there's a chance to resist, you know, before there's even a notion of pulling out their wallet. So that's a big one that we're going to talk a lot about when we when we look at the confirmation stage. Next thing, belief in outcome, you know, that's the next big milestone. It's this steadfast belief that the outcome is possible. You know, excitement and commitment can indeed exist before the unshakable belief that it's possible. And that's something we call hope. You know, they hope it will happen. But by this stage in your timeline, after you've given the arguments and the reasons why it's possible, you know, we are turning that inspiration to aspiration. You know, the dream has become more lucid. They can project themselves in that future reality or outcome, not just all the way on the other end of the spectrum, but actually doing it, taking the tangible practical steps that your program will later facilitate in order to make that outcome possible. So we do this through a series of a ha's insights, beliefs and reframes. The pre-launch is really instrumental in delivering this and, you know, just making it more aspirational, going from inspiration to aspiration, which is really a more active force. You know, they see this new version of themselves not on an abstract level, not as an abstract ideal, but a tangible real possibilities. You know, they have this version of themselves to grow into, which your product will later facilitate. So this is a critical juncture and it's our marketing that's given them the gift of a believable and attainable, attainable aspiration. And this brings us to the next stage, which is the inevitability of the outcome. You know, this is where the journey hits a tipping point and you'll see that on the process map, it's where we start to have that downward slope. And this is where the belief that it's possible turns into an inevitable outcome. And the reason being, because your program, with its unique features and mechanisms, finally comes into play and gives that aspiration a clear strategy and path to manifest. And because they've already pre-committed to achieving it prior in your launch, you know, and have been given a full understanding of why they have and achieved it in the past, they're fueled by this renewed belief that it's possible and your program acts as the final piece to make it inevitable. However, even though this is where we hit that tipping point, not everyone will buy here. You know, the transformation and outcome may be inevitable, but for most, there will still be redoubled resistance, you know, some last minute self sabotage, you know, that this won't work for me attitude, that special snowflake syndrome, if you will. There's going to be that fear of change, you know, investing and buying new products and changing yourself even in an aspirational way is fearful. We're not wired to want to do that. So resistance and skepticism are going to come in strong here. And, you know, in Joseph Campbell parlance, that would be the dark night of the soul moment. This is where most marketers will, you know, counterintuitively say, just try to scare them off by throwing more scarcity and FOMO into the fire, which has its place, but it doesn't address what your prospect really needs here. They're already in this dark, loathsome place, really wrestling with this negativity, wrestling with this resistance. And, you know, there is that place for real urgency, but it's not the only thing, and it's definitely not the only thing that they will need in this moment. What they will really need is safety and the new outcome, you know, and on an internal level, this is where future pacing will be extremely helpful, you know, for them to see what it will look like in the minutes, days, weeks, months and years after they've stepped into that new reality, because there's safety and predictability. So we seek to do our customer, our prospect, the favor of removing the fear of the unknown. We make it known. We future pace what it's going to look like, and we, in doing so, we make the investment feel safe, you know. So when we're making the investment feel safe, we go beyond just a standard guarantee, social proof and case studies. Sure, we use those, but we also tap into some deeper things that create safety for people. And one of the big ones and most underutilized one is we re-empower them to make a choice. You know, we give them a chance to not get pushed past the buyer's line, which is scary and something they would resist, but to actually take that step themselves by their own volition, by taking your hand and making that step themselves. And in module seven, we'll be looking at some very specific techniques and strategies to do that. And then finally, vindication. Swiped from my favorite movie of all time, I call this the Andy Dufresne moment, you know, and this picture is kind of better, but you know, most people who've seen this movie will recognize the one with his arms flailing, rain pouring down on him, and just this awesome moment in cinematic history. But that kind of looks like a vindicated smile. So we're going to roll with that for now. But one of the greatest gifts we can give our prospect is that sense of vindication of redemption. You know, whereas we start the journey by verifying and validating their current reality by the end of the process, they should feel this awesome sense of vindication that all aspects of their past, the failures, the doubt, the resistance, the stress, it's all okay. It's all led to this moment. It's a powerful gift we can give them. It's been given meaning. The pain has been of use. It hasn't been wasted. You know, think of the line a lot of, you know, husbands or wives may give each other, you know, when they're talking about their partner, I'm so happy. I finally, finally met you. That word finally is the vindication. It points to all the pain and missteps they've had in previous relationships that it's all okay now. It's been alchemized into a prerequisite for this final success. So this is a really powerful trigger, you know, and we're going to talk a lot more about it when we get towards the end of the course. If you have the six figure emails package, there's an additional template called markets search for meaning, which was actually inspired by Victor Frankel and his process of logotherapy of turning these struggles into those prerequisites for success or just reframing them in a new favorable favorable way that vindicates them. So that's the big one. But, you know, at the end of the day, giving meaning to struggle is one of the most generous gifts we can give another human. And now we have a chance to do it at scale with email marketing. So it's the ultimate win-win. It's the culmination of this entire process. And I really can't wait for you to start leveraging it. And then finally, the last part I want to really kind of hone in on here is you'll see that there are three state checks that we use strategically throughout this coaching the conversion process. And this is essentially the equivalent of when you're having that one-on-one coaching call or a therapy session, and they will ask you, you know, they will get your buy-in again, they will get reaffirmation. Is it okay if I talk about this? Do you want to hear about this? Are you ready to go deeper into this? So it's really getting that invitation again. We spoke before about permission versus invitation marketing. One powerful way to do it is to just ask for that invitation, you know, a few times. So it's just kind of like courtesy and makes people feel like they have control over it. You know, and that was another big element we just spoke about a little earlier is giving people that sense of power, that sense of control, giving them a chance to exercise their own volition as strategic points. So you're going to see how we do that a little later. State check one is all about making sure they're really on board. So that's an email I will send out during the segmentation phase, especially if they haven't signed up for the journey over the first one or two invites. So you'll see a theme plate on how to do that. And then one that I really love leveraging is state check two, right before cart open, you know, getting permission to talk about it. So that's exactly when a coach would say, I've heard everything you've said, I think I have something that can really help. Can I tell you about it? So that's what makes people feel more empowered. It's that micro commitment, it's that buy-in. And then finally, we do it in the close when we're actually trying to get a decision and you'll see a theme plate for that as well. And, you know, I just want to leave you with this with this thought, you know, one of the biggest discrepancies between personal selling and online selling is that one on is that in one on one, the process must end in a choice. You know, if you're doing your follow ups, it doesn't end with people just kind of like falling off the radar. You know, if you're really following up with people, it has to end in a choice. Whereas in online marketing, it ends with a whimper, you know, in action and a passing deadline, which just isn't very informative or valuable for either party. So that's really kind of what the final state check is going to help us with, really asking for that decision. So that's it. That is the journey at a glance. And before we can expect our avatar to embark on it, it's really time to give them that feat, give them a heartbeat, make them real, and to take them off the page as a two dimensional static thing sitting in a Google doc and make them real and vivid and pretty much that best friend you met at Starbucks last weekend. So take a break. This was a really big lesson steep in it for a little bit. And then meet me in lesson two, where we'll actually explore a few really cool exercises to bring your prospect to life. So I will see you there.
